## Prediction Intervals and Quantiles {#sec-intervals}

Prediction intervals and quantiles represent uncertainty by identifying
specific points or bounded ranges in the probability distribution of a future
outcome — communicating not the full shape of the distribution but the most
decision-relevant summaries of its extent. A prediction interval specifies a
range within which a future observation is expected to fall with a stated
probability: a 90% prediction interval, for instance, is constructed to
contain the true future value on approximately 90% of occasions when the
forecast is made under similar conditions [@raftery2016]. A quantile specifies
a threshold below which a given proportion of possible outcomes are expected
to fall: a forecast at the 0.65 quantile, for example, "will estimate a value
that is lower than the observed value 65% of the time" [@amazon_nd].

These two closely related concepts can be presented visually — most commonly
through error bars, box plots, or shaded bands on time series plots — or
numerically, as explicit lower and upper bounds accompanying a central
estimate. In a standard box plot, a central rectangle represents the middle
quartiles (the 25th to 75th percentile range), a line inside the box indicates
the median, and extending whiskers represent upper and lower probability bounds
[@efsa2019]. In time series forecasting, prediction intervals are typically
displayed as a shaded region surrounding the central forecast line, widening
as the horizon extends to reflect the accumulation of uncertainty over time.

The relationship between prediction intervals and the full distributional
displays described in @sec-distributions is one of summarisation: a prediction
interval extracts two specific quantiles from the full distribution and
presents them without the distributional shape that lies between and beyond
them. This compression is precisely what makes intervals practical for
non-expert audiences — and precisely what creates their characteristic
limitations, as the information that is discarded by the compression is not
always negligible.

### Practical Example

Prediction intervals have been applied across a wide range of forecasting
contexts. In weather forecasting, the University of Washington's Probcast
website communicated temperature uncertainty to the general public using the
10th and 90th percentiles of the forecast distribution — an 80% prediction
interval — alongside the probability of specific adverse weather events
[@raftery2016]. The choice of 80% rather than 95% intervals was deliberate:
while 95% intervals contain more true outcomes, they are "also much wider and
hence may be judged less useful" for practical decision-making by users
planning outdoor activities or making weather-sensitive choices [@raftery2016].

In epidemiological forecasting during the COVID-19 pandemic, 95% prediction
intervals were used to present probabilistic bounds around time-series
forecasts of hospital bed occupancy, providing health system managers with
an upper bound for capacity planning [@leffrang2025]. In demographic
forecasting, @raftery2023 use prediction intervals around Total Fertility Rate
projections to demonstrate how forecast uncertainty is "narrow for short-term
predictions, then widens and finally stabilises as the forecast horizon
increases" — a pattern that is directly legible from the interval display
without requiring the viewer to interpret a full distributional shape.

In intelligence analysis, @mandel2021 advocate for replacing vague linguistic
probability terms with explicit numeric credible intervals: reporting that
there is a "65% to 85% probability that a given event will occur" is more
precise, more verifiable, and less susceptible to motivated misinterpretation
than verbal alternatives such as "likely."

In retail and supply chain management, the quantile framework is applied
directly to asymmetric cost structures: when "the cost of being understocked
is often higher than the cost of being overstocked," planning at the 75th
quantile rather than the median is more economically appropriate, because it
provides protection against the higher-cost error at an acceptable frequency
[@amazon_nd].

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Annotated Box-and-Whisker Plot**

Suggested figure: Figure 2 in @efsa2019, "Example of a box-and-whisker plot
for the average prevalence of pregnant slaughtered animals," which explicitly
annotates the 50% certainty interval (the box), the 98% certainty interval
(the whiskers), and the median (the central line), providing an exemplary
template for a labelled interval display.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @efsa2019.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Meteorological Prediction Interval Band**

Suggested figure: Figure 5B in @spiegelhalter2011, "95% prediction intervals
produced by the UK Meteorological Office for the maximum temperature expected
for 5 days," showing a standard interval band overlaying a line chart of
forecast temperatures.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @spiegelhalter2011 or from
publicly available UK Met Office forecast archives.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Epidemiological Prediction Interval**

Suggested figure: Figure 4 in @leffrang2025, showing a 95% prediction interval
plot for the forecast of COVID-19 hospitalisations, providing a modern example
of a predictive interval band mapped onto a time series in a public health
context.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @leffrang2025.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Wind Speed Box Plot with Prediction Intervals**

Suggested figure: Figure 4 in @nadav2008, displaying a customised box plot
showing median wind speed inside a box representing the 50% prediction
interval, with whiskers representing the 80% prediction interval — a format
specifically designed and tested for weather forecast communication.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @nadav2008.
:::

### Strengths and Limitations

**Strengths.** Prediction intervals have several important practical advantages.
First, they facilitate risk management under conditions of asymmetric costs.
Because quantiles can be specified to match the cost structure of a particular
decision — providing more protection against the more costly type of error —
they enable decision-makers to optimise their planning in a way that a simple
central estimate cannot support [@amazon_nd]. The "risk avoider" identified
by @raftery2016 — a user whose primary goal is to limit the probability of
an adverse outcome below an acceptable threshold — finds a specific quantile
far more actionable than the full distributional information from which it
is derived.

Second, visualising uncertainty through box plots and error bars enables users
to extract mean estimates and make subsequent decisions with higher accuracy
than complex distributional displays. @edwards2012 found that "error bar
displays and box plots work best for mean estimation and choice of action" —
a finding that supports the use of intervals precisely in contexts where
simplicity and actionability are priorities.

Third, communicating uncertainty through numerical ranges with a point estimate
does not significantly reduce public trust in the source. @vanderbles2020
demonstrate that this format "does not substantially decrease trust in either
the numbers or the source" — directly addressing one of the most common
concerns among institutional communicators who worry that expressing
uncertainty will damage their credibility.

Fourth, quantile-based communication offers a practical solution to the
challenge of expert knowledge communication. @raftery2016 demonstrate that
a single, well-chosen quantile can serve as the entire actionable output of
a complex probabilistic analysis — as in the International Whaling Commission's
use of the fifth percentile of the bowhead whale replacement yield distribution
as a precautionary quota limit — delivering probabilistically principled
decisions without requiring the audience to interpret a full distribution.

**Limitations.** The most persistent and well-documented limitation of
prediction intervals is what @toet2016 call the *uniform distribution fallacy*:
when numeric intervals or error bars are presented without explicit information
about distributional shape, audiences routinely assume a uniform distribution,
incorrectly believing that all values within the range are equally probable.
As @spiegelhalter2011 describe it, the visual presentation of an interval
makes it appear as though "values within the interval are equally likely,"
encouraging users to anchor on the extreme bounds rather than the central
estimate — even though those extremes represent the least likely outcomes
within the interval.

A related limitation is excessive focus on the extremes. Because the bounds
of an interval are visually salient — they are the edges of a bar or the
endpoints of a whisker — users tend to anchor on them, producing a systematic
bias toward the consideration of extreme scenarios at the expense of the more
probable central outcomes [@spiegelhalter2011].

A third limitation is the *deterministic construal error* in its interval-
specific form: audiences frequently interpret the boundaries of a prediction
interval as strict categorical partitions, perceiving values within the
interval as "accurate" and values outside it as "erroneous" — as if the
interval were a binary classifier rather than a probabilistic statement
[@leffrang2025]. This misinterpretation can lead to inappropriate surprise
and loss of trust when an actual outcome falls outside a stated interval,
even if that outcome had a non-trivial probability.

Finally, because box plots and ranges are also widely used to display
population variability, audiences routinely misinterpret epistemic uncertainty
intervals as descriptions of real-world heterogeneity — the same confusion
documented for distributional displays in @sec-distributions [@efsa2019].

**Disagreement in the literature.** The literature reveals a historical
disagreement about how numeric intervals affect perceived competence and trust.
@vanderbles2020 conclude from meta-analysis that providing numeric ranges does
not negatively affect trust, while earlier research by Johnson and Slovic
(referenced in @vanderbles2020) found that interval communication "signalled
honesty and competency for some participants, but dishonesty and incompetency
for others." A second tension concerns the visual design of interval
boundaries: while traditional statistical graphics use sharp, crisp boundary
lines for intervals, @toet2016 argue that "blurred or dashed (versus sharp)
borders" would better convey that values become gradually less probable as
they approach the extremes of the interval, reducing the deterministic
construal error. This recommendation has not yet been widely adopted in
practice.

### When to Use This Method

Prediction intervals and quantile forecasts are appropriate across a wide
range of audiences and contexts, making them among the most versatile methods
in the uncertainty communicator's toolkit. For risk avoiders who need to
guarantee an outcome up to a specific probability threshold, a specific
quantile — chosen to match the cost structure of the decision — is the
appropriate output [@raftery2016]. For mass-media communications regarding
scientific facts, numerical intervals with a central point estimate represent
"the most robust format for conveying uncertainty without confusing the general
public" [@vanderbles2020].

The choice of coverage probability matters. @raftery2016 recommend 80%
prediction intervals over the conventional 95% for public-facing
communications, on the grounds that narrower intervals are more useful for
practical decision-making even though they contain fewer actual outcomes.
For high-stakes decisions where the tail risk is the primary concern — flood
protection, capacity planning, infrastructure resilience — wider intervals
or specific extreme quantiles may be more appropriate.

Intervals should be preferred over full distributional displays when the
audience is at the entry or informed level, when the communication context
does not support the cognitive load of distributional interpretation, or when
the specific decision can be mapped onto a single probability threshold.

### Good Practice

- **Always state the associated probability explicitly:** Never present a
  numeric range in isolation. Always pair the range with a clear statement
  of the probability that the quantity falls within it — for example, "there
  is a 90% probability that the outcome will fall between X and Y"
  [@efsa2019].
- **Always provide the central estimate:** Accompany every interval with a
  median or mean estimate to counter the tendency of users to focus on the
  extremes and to signal whether the distribution is symmetric or skewed
  [@efsa2019].
- **Incorporate density cues where possible:** To reduce the uniform
  distribution fallacy, supplement intervals or box plots with a visual
  density strip or explicit text explaining that central values are more
  probable than extreme values [@efsa2019].
- **Consider blurred or dashed interval borders:** To reduce the deterministic
  construal error, explore the use of visual treatments — such as gradual
  fading at the interval boundaries — that signal the continuous nature of
  the probability distribution beyond the stated bounds [@toet2016].
- **Communicate interval bounds explicitly in text:** Do not require audiences
  to read the bounds of an interval from a graphical axis. Always state the
  upper and lower bounds numerically in accompanying text so that the precise
  values are unambiguous [@efsa2019].
- **Always accompany box plots with explanations:** Because box plots are
  frequently misunderstood, include a textual or graphical key that explicitly
  defines what the whiskers, box, and central line represent, and reiterate
  that the graphic represents epistemic uncertainty about a single quantity
  rather than population variability [@efsa2019].
- **Match the quantile to the decision:** When advising specific users rather
  than a general audience, choose the quantile to communicate based on the
  cost structure of their decision — protecting against the high-cost error
  at an appropriate frequency rather than defaulting to the conventional 50th
  or 95th percentile [@amazon_nd; @raftery2016].
